era_context: Late Bronze Age, Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. Ramesses III's reign continues in Egypt. The Hittite Empire is in its final phase, with Suppiluliuma II still ruling but facing mounting pressures. Mycenaean Greece remains in the palatial period. The international Bronze Age system is functioning but showing signs of stress. Climate records suggest increasing aridity in some regions. This is a period of transition, though the full scale of the coming collapse is not yet apparent to contemporaries.

events.title: Mycenaean palatial culture at its height
events.description: The Mycenaean palatial centres of mainland Greece—Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos, and others—are at the height of their power and influence. Linear B texts document a complex administrative system, military organisation, and religious practices. The palaces are centres of craft production, trade, and political authority. This is the Late Helladic IIIB period, characterised by monumental architecture, sophisticated pottery, and extensive trade networks. Within a generation, these palaces will be destroyed and the palatial system will collapse.

sources.name: Linear B tablets from Pylos, Mycenae, and Tiryns

events.certainty_note: The existence and prosperity of Mycenaean palatial centres in the late 13th century BCE is confirmed by extensive archaeological evidence and Linear B texts. The assignment to -1676 is conventional, as the palatial period spans several decades.

events.title: Shang dynasty in China continues under late Shang rule
events.description: The Shang dynasty continues its rule in China, with the late Shang period characterised by increasingly elaborate bronze-working, oracle bone divination, and complex administrative structures. The Shang kings maintain control over the Yellow River valley and surrounding regions. This period is well-documented through oracle bones, bronze inscriptions, and archaeological evidence. The Shang dynasty will fall to the Zhou conquest within a few decades.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Anyang and other Shang sites

events.certainty_note: The existence and activities of the late Shang dynasty are confirmed by oracle bones and bronze inscriptions. However, the absolute dating of individual years within the Shang period is uncertain, with scholarly debate over whether the Shang dynasty ended circa 1046 BCE or earlier. The assignment to -1676 follows conventional Shang chronology.

disconfirming_evidence: The Mycenaean palatial system is sometimes presented as stable and prosperous in this period, but evidence suggests underlying stress: Linear B texts document military preparations, resource management problems, and possibly labour unrest. The collapse of the palaces may have been triggered by external invasion (the traditional 'Dorian invasion' hypothesis), but modern scholarship emphasises systemic failure, climate change, and internal instability. The Shang dynasty's dating is highly contested; some scholars place the Shang-Zhou transition as early as 1050 BCE, while others argue for dates as late as 1000 BCE or even later.
historiographic_note: The Mycenaean period is well-documented through Linear B texts and archaeology, but the texts are primarily administrative and do not provide narrative history. The perspective is that of the palatial elite; the lives of ordinary people are largely invisible. For the Shang dynasty, oracle bones provide a window into religious and political concerns, but they are fragmentary and often difficult to interpret. The absolute chronology of the Shang is one of the most debated issues in Chinese history, with implications for synchronising Chinese and Near Eastern chronologies.

graph_edges.note: Mycenaean palatial prosperity precedes the collapse of the palace system
